coredata.dc:title: Photoelectric biomemristors for artificial visual perception systems
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lu
Li, Jiazhuang
Su, Wantao
Wen, Dianzhong
coredata.dc:description: The redundant information generated by traditional image sensors causes a large amount of energy waste, which can no longer meet the needs of the rapid development of current artificial intelligence. Therefore, it is urgent to develop visual bionic systems with perception, memory and processing functions. Photoelectric memristors based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the body fluid of Bombyx mori (BM) were fabricated, which can change its storage state when triggered by optical or electrical signals, and synaptic characteristics such as multilevel data storage, synaptic potentiation and synaptic depression were realized. The Boolean logic "OR" operation, the enhancement/inhibition of the synapse under the light signal and the double pulse dissimilation behavior are realized by UV light-driven resistance variable memory. The results show that photoelectric memristors have attractive biological applications in information perception, storage and computation and provide a potential method for the next generation of artificial visual perception systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectric memristors
Synaptic behavior
Photoelectric or gate

National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62371183 ), the Heilongjiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. LH2021F045 ) and the Basic Research Project funded by the Basic Research Business Funding for Provincial Universities in Heilongjiang Province (Grant No. 2022-KYYWF-1088 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Physical picture of Bombyx mori. (b) Structure of the Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass device diagram. (c) Cross-section SEM image. (d) TEM image of BM. (e) UV‒visible spectra of the BM film. (f) UV‒visible spectra of the GQD film.

Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO, Al/BM:GQDs/ITO, and Al/BM/ITO devices: (a-c) I-V characteristic curve, (d-f) repeatability test, (g-i) switching current ratio, (j-l) threshold voltage distribution, (m-o) retention time, and (p-r) durability test.

Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO, Al/BM:GQDs/ITO, and Al/BM/ITO devices: (a-c) Resistance cumulative probability. (d-f) HRS/LRS distribution of different cells. (g-i) Double logarithmic curve. (j-m) Conductivity mechanism diagram.

Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO devices: (a) I-V characteristic curves under different compliance currents; (b) Current values under HRS and LRS; (c) Effect of UV light time on I-V characteristics of devices; (d) Effect of different unit areas on I-V characteristics of devices; (e) Relationship between synapses and RRAM in organisms; (f) Conductivity response under single pulse stimulation; (g) Repeatability of device conductance modulation; (h) PPF behavior; (i) Potentiation and depression behavior.

(a) Schematic diagram of ultraviolet light. (b-c) Device mechanism under ultraviolet light. (d) I-V characteristic change of the device under ultraviolet light. (e) Current response of the device under different input conditions. (f) OR gate and its photoelectric logic truth table.
The structures and characteristic parameters of some biomemristors.
Threshold voltage distribution of the three devices.

head.ce:title: Photoelectric biomemristors for artificial visual perception systems

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic Engineering, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Electronic Engineering, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Electronic Engineering, Heilongjiang University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150080, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The redundant information generated by traditional image sensors causes a large amount of energy waste, which can no longer meet the needs of the rapid development of current artificial intelligence. Therefore, it is urgent to develop visual bionic systems with perception, memory and processing functions. Photoelectric memristors based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the body fluid of Bombyx mori (BM) were fabricated, which can change its storage state when triggered by optical or electrical signals, and synaptic characteristics such as multilevel data storage, synaptic potentiation and synaptic depression were realized. The Boolean logic "OR" operation, the enhancement/inhibition of the synapse under the light signal and the double pulse dissimilation behavior are realized by UV light-driven resistance variable memory. The results show that photoelectric memristors have attractive biological applications in information perception, storage and computation and provide a potential method for the next generation of artificial visual perception systems.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the human biological system, the sensory nervous system dominates various sensory nervous tissues in the human body [1–3]; it uses peripheral nerve endings to perceive external information such as light, touch and hearing and transform it into biological signals stored in the human brain and to deal with this information [4–6], including human visual perception, which is the main way to obtain information. The eye is a sensory organ that recognizes light signals and provides visual information, such as distance, color, and shape. The most important function of the visual perception system is the recognition, storage and computation of various kinds of light. Therefore, it is very urgent to study photosensitive devices with high light sensitivity, fast current response speed and low threshold voltage [7]. Before the visual perception system usually used optical sensors as artificial retinas [8–10] and modulus conversion circuits as the path of the connection calculation, some of the neural network algorithms dealt with because each part consumes large amounts of time and power [11–14] and is responsible for a large amount of wasted resources; therefore, there is an urgent need to break through the bottleneck of this field. Many researchers at home and abroad have made good progress, but there are still some problems to be solved [15,16]. First, there are more studies on ultraviolet light, while visible light is less studied, which limits the practical application of artificial visual perception systems. Second, the sensitivity of the optical response current of the device is low, and the sensitivity of the system to the response current needs to be improved through the peripheral circuit. Therefore, it is of great research significance to develop a new device that integrates optical perception (visible light and invisible light), storage (data) and calculation (logical operation and neural computation, etc.) so that the artificial visual perception system can effectively avoid wasting resources in the process of data conversion and transmission.

Compared with traditional electrical signals, optical signals have the characteristics of a wide wavelength range, easy parallel input, high precision and fast reaction speed [17–19], and they are often used to detect velocity, acceleration and whether there is damage on the surface and inside of the object. Therefore, research on new electronic devices based on photoelectric stimulation is an important way to develop efficient in-memory computing systems, which attracts more research attention. As the core component of optically controlled artificial visual perception systems, photoelectric memristors have shown great potential in sensing, data storage and neuromorphic computing of the brain [20–22]. For example, photoelectric memristors have been used as a new photoelectric switch and memory element in optoelectronic integrated circuits [23] and optoelectronic neuromorphic computing [24,25], which improves the compactness of the integrated circuit design layout, reduces power consumption and improves the computing speed in neural morphology computing. In terms of optical biogenetics [26–28], neurons of organisms can be modified by light-controlled genes to form channels for the release of light-sensitive particles, and optically controlled neuronal behavior can be studied by changes in calcium ions, vesicle release, neurotransmitters and membrane potential. Photoelectric memristors not only combine the characteristics of resistance variable memory but also have dynamic behavior similar to that of synaptic neurons [29–31]. Therefore, photoelectric memristors are one of the basic devices in future research on artificial visual perception systems.

Biological materials have the advantages of being natural, renewable, easy to obtain, nontoxic and pollution-free and have the characteristics of biocompatibility and degradation in the natural environment, so biological materials have been widely studied and used to make various electronic devices, such as resistive memory and sensors. Resistive memory made of fibroin [32], starch [33], lignin [34], banana peel [35], lotus leaves [36] and other biological materials has bipolar storage characteristics. For example, the Ag/ fibroin/Au device made of fibroin as the medium layer has the data storage characteristics of a switching current ratio of 60, repeatable switching 100 times, and a retention time of 500 s [32]. The Au/starch/ITO device made of potato starch as the medium layer has the characteristics of programmable memory with a switching current ratio of 103 and a retention time of 104 s [33]. The Au/lignin/ITO device made with lignin as the medium layer has the characteristics of multistage data storage with a switching current ratio of 102, repeatable switching of 100 times and a retention time of 103 s [34]. The Ag/banana peel/Ti device made of banana peel as the dielectric layer material has the characteristics of nonvolatile data storage with a switching current ratio of 20∼60 and a retention time of 3 × 104 s [35]. The Ag/Lotus leaves/ITO device prepared with lotus leaves as the medium layer has the characteristics of data storage with a switching current ratio of 40, repeatable switching 100 times and a retention time of 103 s [36]. The structures and characteristic parameters of some published devices using biomaterials as the dielectric layer of the memristor are shown in Table 1 . It can be seen that the proposed memristor with BM as the dielectric layer has a higher switching current ratio, retention characteristics and repeatability and a lower threshold voltage than other biomemristors. Therefore, BM is used as the main material of the dielectric layer of the memristor in this paper.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are emerging organic photocatalysts with high electrical activity and visible light activity. It has good optical stability and can be widely used in photovoltaic devices [37], biological development imaging [38], fluorescence probes [39,40], biological diagnosis [41], solar cells [42] and other fields. In 2018, Xiaobing Yan et al. fabricated a device with a structure of Ag/Ga2O3/PbS QD/Ga2O3/Pt through self-assembled PbS quantum dots and improved the related performance parameters of the device [43]. In 2021, Shuxia Ren et al. made Ag/GQD:GO/ITO devices with a sandwich structure and investigated the effect of GQDs on devices, which can enhance the local electric field and guide the growth direction of conductive wires, thus providing superior resistive switching performance uniformity [44].

In this paper, GQDs are used to fabricate an Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO device. The device has nonvolatile storage capacity, a large switching current ratio and high switching consistency when stimulated by a photoelectric pulse. The device achieves multilevel information storage, synaptic potentiation and depression under the stimulation of electrical pulses. Under the stimulation of ultraviolet light, the photoelectric effect occurs in the dielectric layer, and the photogenerated carriers increase, which makes the device change from the HRS to the LRS. The paired-pulse facilitation behavior (PPF) is realized by using this behavior. The device can perform "OR" gate calculations and simulate synaptic enhancement and inhibition under both optical and electrical excitation.

In this paper, Al/BM/ITO/glass, Al/BM:GQDs/ITO/glass and Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass devices were fabricated. BM was derived from the body fluid of Bombyx mori. GQDs were obtained from Suzhou Hengqiu Technology Co., Ltd., and PMMA was obtained from Tianjin Fengboat Chemical Reagent Factory.

First, a glass sheet with an ITO electrode was placed into a clean beaker and cleaned with acetone, alcohol and deionized water by sonication for 15 min. The BM solution was taken from the silkworm and cut through the antennae of its abdomen. Its body fluid will automatically flow out. The BM solution and GQDs solution were mixed at a ratio of 3:1 and sonicated for 30 min. The PMMA solution with a concentration of 100 mg/ml was prepared by dissolving 1 g of PMMA powder in 10 ml of anisole and magnetic stirring for more than 12 h at room temperature. Second, the BM:GQDs solution was divided into two parts: one part was irradiated with 395 nm ultraviolet light for 10 min and 30 min, and then the BM:GQDs solution was applied to ITO glass by spin coating at speeds of 500 rpm (5 s) and 4000 rpm (40 s) and dried at 105 °C for 10 min. The other part of the BM:GQDs solution was directly applied to ITO glass by spin coating under the above conditions. Finally, an Al top electrode was deposited on the dielectric layer by the vacuum evaporation method and annealed at 105 °C for 10 min to complete the device fabrication. The device structure was Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO.

The electrical test was carried out by using a semiconductor parameter instrument (Keithley 4200, Solon). Scanning electron microscopy (Hitachi s3400, Hitachi) was used to observe the cross-section of the ITO glass substrate coated with Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA ITO.

Fig. 1(a) is a physical photo of Bombyx mori. Fig. 1(b) is a schematic diagram of the structure of Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO devices, in which Al, PMMA, BM:GQDs, PMMA and ITO layers are arranged from top to bottom. Fig. 1 (c) is an SEM photo of the device cross-section, in which the thickness of each layer is approximately the top electrode Al (63 nm), insulation layer PMMA (24 nm), dielectric layer BM:GQDs (30 nm), insulation layer PMMA (33 nm) and low electrode ITO (63 nm). Fig. 1(d) shows a typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the BM. A spherical structure with a diameter of 30∼40 nm can be observed in Fig. 1(d). Fig. 1(e) and Fig. 1(f) show the UV‒visible absorption spectra of BM and GQDs, respectively. The absorption peak edges of the BM film and GQD film are located at wavelengths of 437 nm and 450 nm, respectively. According to the formula Eg=hc/λ, the bandgap widths of the BM and GQDs are Eg=2.84 eV and Eg=2.76 eV, respectively.

Fig. 2 shows the electrical characteristics of three devices: Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO, Al/BM:GQDs/ITO, and Al/BM/ITO. Fig. 2 (a-c) shows the I-V characteristic curves of the three devices, indicating that the device has good bipolar resistance switching behavior. The voltage scanning direction of the curves is +5 V → 0 V →−5 V → 0 V → +5 V, and the scanning sequence is 1 → 4 in Fig. 2 (a-c). Fig. 2 (d-f) shows the repeatability tests of three devices, and the results show that the three devices can be repeated more than 100 times, with Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass devices repeating more than 150 times (Fig. 2(d)). The incorporation of GQDs improves the switching current ratio of the device, while the addition of PMMA barrier layers improves the repetition times of the device, making the device more reliable. Fig. 2(g-i) shows the maximum switching current ratios of the three devices. The switching current of BM-based devices is relatively low, with a maximum of 1.86 × 103 (Fig. 2(i)). The switching current ratio of BM:GQDs devices increases, with a maximum value of 1.55 × 105 (Fig. 2(h)), and the switching current ratio of devices inserted with PMMA layers above and below the BM:GQDs dielectric layer is not significantly different from that of BM:GQDs devices, with a maximum of 9.33 × 104 (Fig. 2(g)). Fig. 2(j-r) shows the threshold voltage distribution, retention time and durability test of the Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO, Al/BM:GQDs/ITO, and Al/BM/ITO devices. Fig. 2(j-l) is the histogram of the threshold voltage distribution based on 100 times the I-V characteristic curve of the three devices, and the table shown in Table 2 is drawn based on these data. It can be concluded that although the switching current ratio of the BM device increases with the addition of GQDs, the threshold voltage (VSET) tends to increase. Therefore, the PMMA barrier layer is added on the original basis, which not only significantly reduces the voltage of the device VSET and VRESET but also reduces the distribution range of the device threshold voltage. Fig. 2(m-o) shows the retention time of the three devices, indicating that the high/low resistance state (HRS/LRS) can be held for more than 104 s, which proves that the device has a stable storage state. Fig. 2(p-r) shows the durability test of the device. The results show that although the HRS fluctuates slightly, it has little impact, which proves that the device has a long-term stable data storage window.

Fig. 3 (a-c) shows the cumulative resistance probabilities of the three devices. The cumulative coefficients of variation of the LRS resistance are 0.06, 0.20 and 0.10, and the cumulative coefficients of variation of the HRS resistance are 0.32, 0.50 and 0.42. The resistance values of the HRS/LRS devices are improved to varying degrees. To test the uniformity of the device, the cyclic volt-ampere characteristics of 5 cells in each device are randomly tested, and Fig. 3(d-f) is drawn by using their current values at 1 V, which further confirms that the device has good consistency, uniformity and repeatable characteristics. It can be further concluded that the incorporation of GQDs makes the HRS resistance of the device fluctuate slightly (Fig. 3(e)), but the HRS resistance of the device becomes more stable with the addition of the PMMA barrier layer (Fig. 3(d)). Fig. 3(g-i) shows the typical I-V characteristic curves of Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO, Al/BM:GQDs/ITO and Al/BM/ITO devices redrawn in double logarithmic coordinates under a positive bias voltage. The fitting results show that the slopes of the three devices under LRS are 0.92, 0.97 and 0.99. The voltage and current show a linear relationship (the slope is approximately equal to 1), and the fitting results conform to the ohmic conduction law. Under HRS, the BM device can be divided into two parts: the curve slope of the low voltage part is 1.13, which meets the ohmic conduction. For the high voltage part, the slope of the curve is 1.81. The injected carriers occupy a dominant position and are captured by the charge capture center, which conforms to the space charge limited current theory (SCLC). BM:GQDs devices are divided into three parts: the curve slopes are 1.13, 1.46 and 2.32, which conform to Ohm's law and space charge limited current theory (SCLC). PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA devices are divided into three parts: the curve slopes are 0.91, 1.97 and 2.49, which conform to ohmic conduction and space charge limited current theory (SCLC).

Fig. 3(j-k) shows the energy band diagram of Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO devices, and the band gaps of various materials are marked in Fig. 3(j). The initial state of the device is the HRS, as shown in Fig. 3(j). When a negative voltage of 0→−5 V is applied to the top electrode Al of the device, the energy level of the device will bend (as shown in Fig. 3(k)), and the electrons in the dielectric layer will obtain enough energy to migrate. BM: GQDs that reach the middle layer through the PMMA barrier layer will be trapped by the gap in the middle layer. When the voltage is increased to VSET, the electrons will break free and pass through the second layer of PMMA to reach the bottom electrode ITO, forming a conductive channel between the two electrodes. The device is converted to the LRS, as shown in Fig. 3(l). Due to the charge capture effect of GQDs, more oxygen-containing groups will be introduced into the dielectric layer to increase the energy band barrier, thus deepening the electron capture depth of the composite film, increasing the initial resistance of the device, and increasing the switching current ratio of the Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO device. When a positive bias voltage of 0 →+5 V is applied to the top electrode Al of the device, the electrons in the BM:GQDs are captured under the effect of the electric field. Until a large positive bias voltage (VRESET) is applied to the device, the state of the device is reversed to the HRS, and the data are electrically erased, as shown in Fig. 3(m).

The VSET of the Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO device after the introduction of GQDs increases because when a negative voltage is applied to the top electrode (Al) of the device, some of the injected electrons are captured by GQDs, so compared with the undoped GQD device, the Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO device requires a larger voltage to form a conductive channel, so VSET increases. After the addition of the PMMA layer, the distribution range of VSET and VRESET of the Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO device is improved because the band gap of the added PMMA is 4.2 eV, which hinders the movement of electrons to a certain extent during device operation, resulting in the accumulation of charge in the active layer and the formation of a local electric field, which reduces the randomness of the formation of conductive channels in the device. Thus, the distribution range of VSET and VRESET is more concentrated, and the stability of the device is increased.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the I-V characteristic curve of Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO devices under different compliance currents. Under different compliance currents, the LRS of the device varies, while the HRS remains almost constant. Under different compliance current modulations, seven LRSs of the device are obtained, which are denoted as seven storage states: "001″, "010″, "011″, "100″, "101″, "110″ and "111″. The HRS of the device is denoted as "000″ storage status. Thus, the device has 8 different states and implements multilevel data storage (8 levels, 3 bits). Fig. 4(b) shows the durability test results of the device under different resistance states. The results show that the device has stable data storage capacity under eight states, which can be used in the memory field for multibit data storage. Based on the sensitivity of graphene quantum dots to ultraviolet light, the BM:GQDs solution was exposed to light for 10 min and 30 min, and the Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO device is shown in Fig. 4(c), indicating that the current switching ratio of the device changed from 105 to 103 under light conditions. Ultraviolet light increases the number of carriers in the dielectric layer and makes it easier for the conductive channel of the device to form. Through the study of different storage cell areas, it was found that the LRS resistance value of the device was not affected by the device area, and the device was fabricated. The I-V characteristic curve of the device shows that the HRS resistance value fluctuates slightly, as shown in Fig. 4(d).

Due to the similar physical structure between biological synapses and RRAM devices, RRAM devices have received great attention in artificial neural networks. Biological synapses in the human brain transmit information from one neuron to another connected neuron. Fig. 4(e) shows the components of the synapses in the human brain, mainly including presynaptic cells, postsynaptic cells, nuclei, dendrites, cell bodies and axons. When the stimulation, such as action potential, reaches the preneuron, the neurotransmitter will be enhanced and released, leading to an increase in the concentration of Ca2+ ions in the channel and the enhancement of the internal conductance. Therefore, the transconductance of the poststimulation neuron will be enhanced. This process is called synaptic plasticity and is believed to be responsible for the learning and memory operations of the human brain. Here, the device based on Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO can be used as a synaptic device because it is a two-port device and simulates the increase in neurotransmitters through the increase in the number of electron hole pairs when electrical stimulation is applied. The top electrode (Al) is regarded as one end of the presynaptic neuron, and the bottom electrode (ITO) is regarded as one end of the postsynaptic neuron. The increasing amplitude of the pulse voltage was used to simulate the enhancement and inhibition of synapses. Fig. 4(f) is a schematic diagram of the conductance changes of Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO devices under pulse voltage stimulation. When simulating the enhancement behavior, voltage pulse stimulation of − 0.20 V to − 0.70 V (step is 0.05 V), a pulse width of 100 μs and a pulse interval of 10 μs are applied to the device, and the device conductivity is increased. The long-term potentiation (LTP) of synapses is successfully simulated. To simulate the suppression behavior, a voltage pulse stimulus of 1.20 V to 0.30 V (step is 0.05 V) with a pulse width of 100 μs and a pulse interval of 10 μs is applied to the device. The conductivity of the device is reduced, and the long-term depression (LTP) of synapses is successfully simulated. Fig. 4(g) shows the conductance change of the device under continuous pulse stimulation, and the conductivity shows a continuous increasing and decreasing trend. The conductance of the device is continuously adjustable, similar to the characteristics of biological synapses, and can be used in neural morphology computing and neural networks.

Fig. 4(h) shows the current change of the device when light pulse stimulation is applied to the device. The interval between the first light pulse stimulation and the second light pulse stimulation was 20 s, successfully simulating the double pulse dissimilation (PPF) characteristics. The reason for this phenomenon is that when the first light pulse stimulation ends, the number of electron hole pairs generated by light recombines at a slow speed. Applying the second light pulse before complete recombination will lead to the reaccumulation of electrons, making the current of the device increase slowly, which conforms to the definition of PPF:
(1)
PPF =
A 1
/
A 2
× 100 %
where A1 and A2 are the currents when the first and second light pulses are applied, respectively. By designing continuous light pulses and electric pulse stimulation, we can simulate the enhancement and inhibition behavior of synapses. Under 395 nm ultraviolet light, the device displays a continuous increase in conductance, and under 2 V voltage stimulation, the device displays a continuous decrease in conductance (Fig. 4(i)), meeting the rule of synaptic potentiation and depression behavior.

Fig. 5 (a) shows a schematic diagram of the Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass optoelectronic device. The first input signal (Input1) represents the voltage signal, The second input signal (Input2) represents the light signal, "1″ represents the light or voltage signal applied to the device, "0″ represents the state of the device when there is no light or voltage signal, and the output current is expressed in the output. For the detection of optical signals, the light source used in this experiment is a laboratory LED UV curing lamp, which emits violet light with a power of 171.42 mW/cm2 and a wavelength of 395 nm. During the experiment, the light source was fixed 20 cm above the device to irradiate the device, and the corresponding optical signal detection experiment was completed. The number of carriers inside the device is adjusted by ultraviolet light to adjust the state of the device. When the ultraviolet beam irradiates the device, the light passes through the PMMA barrier layer and enters the light-sensitive layer BM:GQDs. At this time, the electrons in BM:GQDs and Al obtain energy, in which the capture process will occur, moving toward the bottom electrode ITO and finally being captured at the PMMA/ITO interface, as shown in Fig. 5(b). Because of electron migration, the potential barrier at the interface of BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO is lowered, which changes the state of the device, and the sharp increase in current makes the device LRS. When ultraviolet light is removed, it is difficult for the captured electrons to return to their original positions. The device will continue to remain in the LRS, proving that the device has the ability to store data for a long time. By applying voltage excitation to the device, the state of the device is switched back to the HRS to realize the electrical erasure of data, as shown in Fig. 5(c). Fig. 5(d) shows the change in device state under light conditions. Under no light conditions, the output current of the device is approximately 10−5∼10−6 A. Suddenly, UV light is applied to the device, and the output current of the device changes significantly, changing to 10−2∼10−3 A. It can be concluded that the device has a certain sensitivity to ultraviolet light. Fig. 5(e-f) shows the current values of Al/PMMA/BM:GQDs/PMMA/ITO devices stimulated by optical signals, electrical signals and optical and electrical signals. The current values of the four states of the device differ greatly, so a current of 10−4 A is taken as the dividing line. When it is greater than or equal to 10−4 A, it is logic "1″, and when it is less than 10−4 A, it is logic "0″. The test results show that when the electrical signal (−2 V) or optical signal (UV light) is input separately to the device, the output current of the device is approximately 5.67 × 10−2 and 5.32 × 10−3 A, corresponding to state "1″. In addition, photoelectric signals are applied to the equipment together, and the output current is approximately 6.03 × 10−2 A, which also corresponds to state "1″. It can be concluded from the table that a single device can complete the operation of the logic OR gate.

In summary, this paper optimizes the performance of BM-based resistive memories by introducing GQDs into the active layer and adding PMMA layers above and below the active layer, which improves the consistency between the ON/OFF current ratio and the threshold voltage of the device, reduces the power consumption of the device, and improves the storage density of the device. Due to the photosensitive property of GQDs, the device can be converted from HRS to LRS under the stimulation of ultraviolet light pulses, and logical OR operation is realized by using two kinds of excitation: optical pulses and electric pulses. The device also has many behaviors similar to biological synapses, including enhancement/inhibition behavior and PPF behavior. The development of photoresist variable memory with optical perception, information storage and computing provides a new choice for applications such as artificial visual perception systems and visual sensing computing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62371183), the Heilongjiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. LH2021F045) and the Basic Research Project funded by the Basic Research Business Funding for Provincial Universities in Heilongjiang Province (Grant No. 2022-KYYWF-1088).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A bioinspired optoelectronically engineered artificial neurorobotics device with sensorimotor functionalities
Nat. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Karbalaei Akbari, S. Zhuiykov, A bioinspired optoelectronically engineered artificial neurorobotics device with sensorimotor functionalities, Nat. Commun., 10 (2019) 3873.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-Powered Synaptic Transistor for Artificial Perception
IEEE Electron Dev. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.K. Li, J. Li, D.L. Jiang, W.H. Fu, W.Q. Zhu, J.H. Zhang, Self-Powered Synaptic Transistor for Artificial Perception, IEEE Electron Dev. Lett., 42 (2021) 1002–1005.
